1: The book opens with its temporal anchor: bîmê šəpōṭ haššōpəṭîm — 'in the days when the judges judged.' The cognate accusative construction ('judges judged') is emphatic, marking a specific era of instability. The famine (rāʿāb) that drives Elimelech to Moab is the first in a chain of depletion: famine → death of husband → death of sons → Naomi's emptiness (v21: ʾănî məlēʾāh hālaḵtî wərêqām hĕšîḇanî YHWH, 'full I went away and empty YHWH has returned me'). The town is Bethlehem — bêt lāḥem, 'house of bread' — with bitter irony: the house of bread has no bread. Elimelech (ʾĕlîmeleḵ, 'my God is king') leaves with his family for Moab, the nation descended from Lot's incest (Genesis 19:37), traditional enemy and the people Ruth will leave to join Israel.
2: The name ʾĕlîmeleḵ ('my God is king') carries the book's central theological theme in miniature: YHWH's sovereignty over famine, death, and restoration. His wife Naomi (noʿomî) derives from nāʿēm (to be pleasant/lovely/sweet), the root that becomes bitter irony when she renames herself māraʾ (bitter) in Ruth 1:20. Their sons Mahlon and Chilion are names that scholars have read as 'sickness' (māḥălāh) and 'failing/annihilation' (kālāh) — possibly ominous names given retrospectively by the narrator, or genuinely the family's names that resonate tragically with the deaths that follow. The family is identified as Ephrathites from Bethlehem of Judah — the same geographical identity given to Jesse (1 Samuel 17:12) and later to the birthplace of the Messiah (Micah 5:2).
8: Naomi's parting blessing uses the book's theological keyword for the first time: yaʿaś YHWH ʿimmāḵem ḥeseḏ — 'may YHWH deal with you in hesed.' The term ḥeseḏ (חֶסֶד) is the most theologically rich word in the Hebrew Bible's vocabulary of relationship: it combines love, loyalty, obligation, and initiative beyond obligation. It is the covenant word — YHWH's fundamental disposition toward his covenant partners (Exodus 34:6: rab-ḥeseḏ, 'abounding in steadfast love'). Naomi invokes it three times in Ruth (Ruth 1:8; Ruth 2:20; Ruth 3:10), each time marking a moment where human covenant loyalty either fails or surpasses expectations. Hesed is both descriptive (what Boaz does) and normative (what YHWH requires and models).
14: The two daughters-in-law respond differently: Orpah 'kissed her mother-in-law' (wattišaq lǎḥǎmôtāh), a farewell act, while Ruth 'clung to her' (dāḇəqāh bāh). The verb dāḇaq (to cling, cleave) is the covenant-commitment verb of Genesis 2:24 — a man 'cleaves' to his wife in marriage covenant. Its use here signals that Ruth's attachment to Naomi is covenantal in character, not merely emotional. The same verb is used for Israel's covenant faithfulness to YHWH (Deuteronomy 10:20; Ruth 11:22; Ruth 13:4 — 'you shall cleave to him'). Ruth's clinging is the book's first enactment of the hesed that will characterize her throughout.
16: Ruth's declaration is one of the most celebrated speeches in the Hebrew Bible for its rhythm, compression, and theological density. Its six clauses form a crescendo: (a) where you go I will go, (b) where you lodge I will lodge, (c) your people will be my people, (d) your God my God, (e) where you die I will die, (f) there I will be buried. The theological center — 'your God my God' — is the formal language of covenant adoption. Ruth is making a legal-religious declaration of affiliation: she is leaving the gods of Moab (cf. Chemosh, Numbers 21:29) and joining the covenant community of YHWH. The Hebrew lacks explicit covenant vocabulary (berît is absent) but the action is covenantal — a Gentile's voluntary inclusion that will define her entire story.
20: Naomi's self-renaming is a rare act of self-denomination in the OT. She exchanges noʿomî (from nāʿēm, pleasant) for māraʾ (bitter), citing šaddai as the agent of her affliction three times in vv20-21: al tiqreʾnâ lî noʿomî qreʾn lî māraʾ kî hēmar lî šaddai — 'Do not call me Naomi; call me Mara, for Almighty has dealt bitterly with me.' El Šaddai is the patriarchal name of God (Genesis 17:1; Ruth 28:3; Ruth 35:11) associated with the Abrahamic covenant promises of descendants and land — the very promises Naomi sees as reversed by her experience. Her lament is formally comparable to the psalms of lament (cf. Ps 88) and to Job, who also names šaddai as his adversary. The bitter irony is that the name she rejects — 'pleasant' — will be exactly what her story becomes by the end.
3: vayyamot elimelech ish naomi: 'Then Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died.' The opening movement of Ruth is a series of deaths: Elimelech (v3), then both sons Mahlon and Chilion (v5). The triple bereavement strips Naomi of every male protector and provider in the patriarchal economy. Elimelech ('my God is king') dies first — the man whose name proclaimed divine sovereignty goes first, leaving the women in a godless economy. The theological irony is deliberate: when 'my God is king' is gone, what remains? The book will answer: chesed (steadfast love), expressed through a Moabite widow and a redeemer kinsman.
4: vayisu lahem nashim moaviyot: 'they took Moabite women as wives.' The sons marry outside Israel — a detail the narrator mentions without editorial judgment. Orpah (possibly from oref, neck/back — later she turns her back) and Ruth (possibly from reut, friendship/companionship, or from the root rvh, to be satisfied) bear names whose meanings become narratively significant. The Moabite origin matters because Ruth's loyalty will explicitly transcend the ethnic boundary her name marks: a Moabitess who becomes the model of chesed is the book's reversal of category expectations.
5: vatishshaer ha-isha mishney yeladeiha ume-ishah: 'she was left without (vatishshaer, was remaining) her two sons and her husband.' The root sh-'-r (to remain, be left over) is the remnant word — the same root as she'erit (remnant) in prophetic literature. Naomi is the survivor, the remnant of her household. The phrase structurally echoes Job 1:15-19, where messenger after messenger says 'I alone have escaped to tell you' (va'imalletah rak ani levaddi). Both texts frame radical loss as the theological starting condition from which YHWH will act. Naomi's emptiness sets the stage for the fullness to come (cf. v21).
6: vataqam hi vekhalloteha vatashav misedei moav: 'Then she arose with her daughters-in-law to return from the fields of Moab, for she had heard in Moab that YHWH had visited his people to give them bread.' The verb qum (to arise) followed by shuv (to return) is a classic departure formula, but shuv here carries dual weight: Naomi is returning home (geographic return) while the book also tracks a spiritual return. The impetus is ki-paqad YHWH et-amo latet lahem lechem — 'YHWH visited his people to give them bread.' The verb paqad (to visit, attend to, muster) is the God-of-Israel's signature action: the same word used for the Exodus visitation (Exodus 4:31). The famine was YHWH's withdrawal; the provision is his return. Naomi's return is embedded in YHWH's return to his people.
7: vatetzeh min hamakom asher hayetah sham: 'she set out from the place where she had been living.' Hamakom (the place) is used without naming Moab directly, creating a deliberate vagueness — the emphasis is on departure, not destination. The phrase ve-shetei khalloteiha imah (and her two daughters-in-law with her) positions the three widows as a fragile unit about to be dissolved. The word derech (road, way) appears — the three are on 'the way to the land of Judah' (lashvot el-eretz yehudah). Ruth is on a literal road and a theological one: a Moabite walking toward the covenant community.
9: yiten YHWH lachem umenuchah ishah beit ishah: 'May YHWH grant each of you rest (menucha) in the house of her husband.' Menucha (rest, settlement, security) is the book's first stated goal for the women. The same word appears in Ruth 3:1 (manoach from the same root) when Naomi asks, 'shall I not seek security/rest for you?' The arc from Naomi's prayer (Ruth 1:9) to her provision-seeking (Ruth 3:1) is the structural spine of the book: the goal is menucha, covenant rest in a stable household, and the entire plot works to deliver it. Theologically, menucha anticipates the ultimate rest in YHWH himself (Psalms 95:11; Hebrews 4:9-11).
10: ki itach nashuv el-amech: 'No — we will return with you to your people.' The daughters-in-law initially resist Naomi's dismissal. The verb shuv (return) creates a paradox: returning with Naomi to Judah is described by the Moabite women as 'returning to your people' (el-amech). But Judah is not their people — it would be their people only through covenant adoption. This verse previews Ruth's ultimate declaration (v16): the loyalty she promises is an act of voluntary covenant embrace, not blood obligation.
11: lamah telaknah imi ha-od li vanim beme'ai: 'Why would you come with me? Do I still have sons in my womb that they might be your husbands?' Naomi's rhetoric reveals her understanding of the levirate system: a widow's security came through marriage to a kinsman. Her point: even if she remarried tonight and bore sons, by the time they grew up the daughters-in-law would be too old. The yibbum (levirate marriage, Deuteronomy 25:5-10) underlying this speech also underlies the goel institution that drives the plot. Naomi uses levirate logic to release her daughters-in-law from obligation, not realizing that the goel institution will achieve what levirate cannot.
12: shuvna velechna ki zaqanti mihyot le-ish: 'Go back, my daughters — I am too old to have a husband.' Naomi's triple imperative (shuvna) — go back, go back, go back — reinforces her attempt to release them from loyalty she considers burdensome. Her phrase ki-zaqanti (I am too old) acknowledges that her reproductive capacity is gone, and therefore the institutional pathways she knows (levirate, kinship marriage) are closed to her. This theological despair is what makes Boaz's action as goel so remarkable: it is not through Naomi's reproductive capacity but through the living Ruth that the line is preserved.
13: lahen al-tenahena banot ki mar-li meod mikkem ki-yatzah bi yad-YHWH: 'Would you wait until they were grown? No, my daughters — my situation is bitterer for you than for me, for the hand of YHWH has gone out against me.' The phrase yad YHWH (hand of YHWH) describes divine agency in affliction — elsewhere it describes the plagues (Exodus 9:3), battlefield defeat, and prophetic commissioning. Naomi's attribution of her suffering to YHWH's active agency is theologically significant: she does not accept random misfortune but acknowledges divine involvement. This is not the bitter fatalism it may seem but a form of faith: she is still addressing YHWH as the author of her circumstances, the same YHWH she later acknowledges as the one who 'has not abandoned chesed to the living and the dead' (Ruth 2:20).
15: hinneh shavah yevimtech el-amah ve'el-eloheiha shuvi acharei yevimtech: 'Look — your sister-in-law has gone back to her people and to her gods. Go back after your sister-in-law.' Naomi's instruction is remarkable: she explicitly mentions Orpah's return 'to her gods' (eloheiha), acknowledging that returning to Moab means returning to Chemosh/Moabite religion. By saying this to Ruth, Naomi is implicitly releasing Ruth from any obligation to YHWH she may have incidentally acquired through marriage. Ruth's subsequent declaration (v16) is therefore a counter to this explicit release — she chooses YHWH's people and YHWH's God despite being formally released from any obligation.
17: baasher tamuti amut vesham ekaver: 'Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried.' The burial commitment is the most radical element of Ruth's vow. In the ancient world, burial in one's ancestral land with one's people was a fundamental identity marker (Genesis 23:1-20; Ruth 47:29-30; Ruth 50:24-25). Ruth voluntarily surrenders her ancestral burial place — her final resting place will be Naomi's, not Moab's. The oath formula koh ya'aseh YHWH li vekhoh yosif ('may YHWH do so to me and more also') is the strongest covenant oath formula in Hebrew, invoking YHWH as guarantor of the commitment. Ruth swears her personal devotion to Naomi by YHWH — a Moabite invoking Israel's God in a personal oath.
18: vatere ki mitametzet hi lalechet ittah vattechdal ledabber eleiha: 'When Naomi saw that she was determined (mitametzet, from ametz, to be firm/strong) to go with her, she stopped urging her.' Ametz is the same root used for Joshua's commissioning: 'Be strong (chazaq) and courageous (ametz)' (Joshua 1:6, 7, 9). The word applied to Ruth's resolution carries military/leadership-quality connotations — her commitment is not sentimental loyalty but firm resolve. Naomi's silence (vattechdal ledabber, she ceased speaking to her about it) is an eloquent concession: the argument is over, Ruth has won it through her own declaration.
19: vatehem kol ha-ir aleihen: 'the whole city was stirred (vatehem) because of them.' The verb hum/hamam (to stir, be in tumult) is used of dramatic arrivals: the same root describes the city of Jerusalem 'in turmoil' at Jesus's entry in Matthew 21:10 (LXX: esesthe). The women of Bethlehem ask: hazot naomi? 'Is this Naomi?' — a question charged with recognition and shock. The woman who left full is returning visibly reduced. The public recognition of her return and diminishment sets up her renaming speech in v20-21.
21: ani mele'ah halachti verekam heshivani YHWH: 'I went out full (mele'ah) and YHWH has brought me back empty (reqam).' The male'/req (full/empty) polarity is this chapter's central contrast and the book's thesis statement in miniature. Naomi went out with husband and two sons — full household. She returns with nothing male. But the narrator knows — and the reader will learn — that Ruth, whom Naomi does not count as compensation, is worth 'more to you than seven sons' (Ruth 4:15). YHWH's economy counts differently than Naomi's self-assessment. The emptiness she names is about to be filled through the very person she discounts. me ah te'anu li veYHWH anah vi veShaddai hera li: 'Why call me Naomi, when YHWH has testified against me, and Shaddai has brought calamity upon me?' — the two divine names used: YHWH (covenant God) and Shaddai (God Almighty, the name associated with patriarchal blessing and curse). The Shaddai name in the ancestral narratives is precisely the God who promises and blesses — Naomi applies the blessing-God in the mode of curse, a bitter reversal.
22: veRut hamoa'viyah kallateha imah hashavah misedei moav: 'So Naomi came back, and with her Ruth the Moabitess, her daughter-in-law, who had returned from the fields of Moab.' The narrative closes the chapter by naming Ruth as 'the Moabitess' — her ethnic identity is foregrounded at each key moment (Ruth 1:22; Ruth 2:2, 6, 21; Ruth 4:5, 10). The repetition is not accidental: the book is tracking a Moabite's incorporation into the covenant people, and the identifier reminds us of the boundary being crossed. vehemah ba'u bethlehem betechilat qetzir se'orim: 'and they arrived in Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.' The timing is narrative providence: the gleaning laws (Leviticus 19:9-10; Deuteronomy 24:19-22) were the legal institution that would provide for Ruth. YHWH times their arrival at the harvest's start — the legal provision for the poor is open.
